Text: Recent advances in neural retrieval have improved access to large scientific collections.
Many biomedical articles contain specialized terminology that is difficult for non-experts to interpret.
Prior work has shown that transformer models can encode useful contextual information.
Manual curation of scholarly metadata remains expensive and time-consuming.
Ecological synthesis often requires combining evidence from heterogeneous study designs.
Existing topic models provide useful exploratory views but can be hard to evaluate for downstream tasks.
Scientific figures frequently contain information that is not repeated in the surrounding text.
Despite progress in automatic indexing, assigning precise subject labels remains challenging.
Previous annotation pipelines relied on keyword matching and simple rule-based filters.
Low agreement among annotators has been reported in several subjective classification tasks.
This study aims to evaluate whether retrieval-augmented generation improves scientific question answering.
We investigate how label imbalance affects agreement measures in text classification.
The goal of the experiment is to compare sentence-level and document-level annotation strategies.
We ask whether short abstracts provide enough context for reliable subject indexing.
This work examines the robustness of large language models on noisy scientific inputs.
Our objective is to identify which process parameters are most frequently reported in experimental papers.
The project seeks to determine whether active learning reduces annotation effort.
To address the limitations of prior benchmarks, we focus on domain-specific scientific corpora.
We compare three prompting strategies using the same manually annotated development set.
We test the hypothesis that expert feedback increases extraction quality over multiple iterations.
Two annotators independently labeled each sentence using a four-category annotation scheme.
We sampled 500 abstracts from open-access publications published between 2020 and 2024.
The model was fine-tuned for three epochs with a learning rate of 2e-5.
Disagreements were resolved after calculating inter-annotator agreement on the initial labels.
We used stratified sampling to maintain a similar number of instances per class.
Each document was converted to plain text and segmented into sentences before annotation.
Performance was measured using macro-F1, accuracy, and Cohen's kappa.
The annotation guidelines were adapted from earlier work on rhetorical role classification.
We designed the experiment to test whether examples in the prompt improve consistency.
The final labels were produced by majority vote across three independent runs.
The retrieval-augmented system answered 18 percent more questions correctly than the baseline.
Annotators agreed on 70 percent of the sentences before discussion.
Macro-F1 increased from 0.61 to 0.74 after adding domain-specific examples.
Most disagreements occurred between the Background and Objective categories.
The active learning strategy reduced the number of labeled examples needed for stable performance.
The model performed best on Method sentences and worst on Background sentences.
Error analysis showed that vague sentences were often assigned inconsistent labels.
These findings support earlier observations that subjective tasks produce lower agreement.
The results suggest that clearer annotation guidelines may improve future reliability.
After calibration, the two annotators reached substantially higher agreement on the test set.
